scenario: You greet at the door.
cultureNote: Greetings are not rushed in Morocco; start with salam before any request.

scenario: You thank them for inviting you.
cultureNote: Thanking the host early shows respect and warmth.

scenario: You say this is your first time in a Moroccan home.
english: This is my first time in a Moroccan home.
cultureNote: This helps hosts understand you may need guidance.

scenario: You ask whether to remove your shoes.
english: Should I take off my shoes?
cultureNote: Some homes expect shoes off; asking is polite.

scenario: You ask permission to come in.
cultureNote: A short permission phrase fits the hospitality setting.

scenario: You reply to the greeting.
cultureNote: Responding to salam is expected and respectful.

scenario: You ask how everyone is.
cultureNote: Use plural when greeting a group.

scenario: You ask if everything is good.
cultureNote: Small wellbeing questions are part of Moroccan greetings.

scenario: You say it is nice to meet them.
cultureNote: A warm sentence helps move from formal to friendly.

scenario: You apologize if you forget a name.
friendlyLatin: smh lia ila nseet chi smiya
arabic: سمح ليا إلا نسيت شي سمية
english: Sorry if I forget a name.
cultureNote: Names can be many in family gatherings; humility is appreciated.

scenario: You ask about the family.
cultureNote: Asking about family is normal and friendly in Morocco.

scenario: You ask about the parents.
cultureNote: Mentioning parents respectfully shows cultural awareness.

scenario: You ask if they have siblings.
english: Do you have brothers or sisters?
cultureNote: This is friendly small talk, but keep it light.

scenario: You ask about the children.
cultureNote: Use this when children are clearly part of the gathering.

scenario: You offer a blessing for a child or relative.
english: May God protect them for you.
cultureNote: Blessings are often appreciated in family talk.

scenario: You say your family is small.
cultureNote: Simple personal details build trust.

scenario: You say you have a brother and a sister.
english: I have a brother and a sister.
cultureNote: Family vocabulary helps conversations feel human.

scenario: You say your parents are fine.
english: My parents are fine, thank God.
cultureNote: Lhamdullah is natural when talking about wellbeing.

scenario: You say you are far from home.
cultureNote: Moroccan hosts may respond warmly when you mention distance.

scenario: You say you talk to them on WhatsApp.
english: I talk with them on WhatsApp.
cultureNote: WhatsApp is a natural bridge for family contact.

scenario: You ask for a little tea.
english: I would like a little tea.
cultureNote: Accepting at least some tea is often polite.

scenario: You compliment the tea.
cultureNote: Tea is a symbol of hospitality, so a compliment is meaningful.

scenario: You ask for no sugar.
cultureNote: Say it gently because Moroccan tea is often sweet.

scenario: You compliment the mint.
cultureNote: Small sensory compliments fit tea culture.

scenario: You thank them for tea.
cultureNote: Thanking for tea is also thanking for hospitality.

scenario: You compliment the house.
cultureNote: Compliment the home respectfully; it honors the host.

scenario: You compliment the riad or sitting space.
cultureNote: Comfort and welcome are part of Moroccan hosting.

scenario: You compliment Moroccan decoration.
cultureNote: This ties language to visual culture.

scenario: You say the atmosphere is warm.
cultureNote: Warmth can mean emotional comfort, not just temperature.

scenario: You bless the host for the effort.
cultureNote: This is a strong Moroccan thank-you for effort or hosting.

scenario: You say you are happy to visit.
english: I am happy to visit you.
cultureNote: A warm opening fits a family visit.

scenario: You thank them for the welcome.
cultureNote: Marhba is not just a word; it is an attitude.

scenario: You ask if the family is well.
cultureNote: This repeats the family-care habit.

scenario: You say the tea was good.
cultureNote: Review phrases should still feel like real speech.

scenario: You say goodbye warmly.
cultureNote: A warm goodbye leaves the door open for future contact.

scenario: You bless the meal.
cultureNote: Bssaha is common around food and drink.

scenario: You compliment the food.
cultureNote: Food compliments matter in Moroccan hospitality.

scenario: You ask where to sit.
cultureNote: Let the host guide seating if you are unsure.

scenario: You ask if you can eat with your hand.
english: Can I eat with my hand?
cultureNote: Right-hand etiquette is important in shared meals.

scenario: You say everything is tasty.
cultureNote: A simple positive comment is better than silence.

dayTitle: قبول أو رفض المزيد من الأكل
scenario: You say you are full.
cultureNote: Hosts may insist; keep your tone warm.

dayTitle: قبول أو رفض المزيد من الأكل
scenario: You accept a little more.
english: Add a little for me, please.
cultureNote: A little acceptance can feel respectful.

dayTitle: قبول أو رفض المزيد من الأكل
scenario: You ask not to fill too much.
english: Please do not give me too much.
cultureNote: This softens refusal while respecting generosity.

dayTitle: قبول أو رفض المزيد من الأكل
scenario: You ask if there is something without meat.
friendlyLatin: wach kayn shi haja bla lhem?
arabic: واش كاينة شي حاجة بلا لحم؟
cultureNote: Dietary needs should be expressed politely and early.

dayTitle: قبول أو رفض المزيد من الأكل
scenario: You say you liked it but cannot eat more.
english: I liked it, but I cannot eat more.
cultureNote: This protects the host from feeling rejected.

scenario: You ask if you can help.
cultureNote: Offering help is kind, even if the host refuses.

scenario: You ask where to put something.
cultureNote: Useful after food, gifts, bags, or dishes.

scenario: You offer to bring water.
cultureNote: Small useful help is better than taking over.

scenario: You ask to leave the cups.
english: Should I leave the glasses here?
cultureNote: This helps avoid moving things wrongly.

scenario: You say you do not want to leave them alone with work.
english: I do not want to leave you working alone.
cultureNote: This shows care while accepting the host’s decision.

scenario: You greet an older man respectfully.
cultureNote: Aami is respectful for an older man; use context carefully.

scenario: You greet an older woman respectfully.
cultureNote: Khalti can sound warm and respectful for an older woman.

scenario: You say you want to listen.
english: I want to listen to you.
cultureNote: Listening is valued in elder conversations.

scenario: You thank them for advice.
cultureNote: Advice often comes with care; thank it.

scenario: You bless them with long life.
english: May God give you long life.
cultureNote: Use blessings sincerely and respectfully.

scenario: You ask a child their name.
cultureNote: Keep questions simple and friendly with children.

scenario: You ask their age.
cultureNote: Useful for family small talk.

scenario: You ask if they understand you.
cultureNote: This can make the child part of the interaction.

scenario: You ask if they want to play.
cultureNote: Use only when appropriate and with family comfort.

scenario: You bless the child.
cultureNote: Blessings for children are common and appreciated.

scenario: You ask to take a photo.
cultureNote: Always ask before photos in homes or with family.

scenario: You ask for a photo with them.
english: I would like a photo with you all.
cultureNote: Photos can be warm memories if consent is clear.

scenario: You respect someone who does not want a photo.
cultureNote: Respecting privacy is part of cultural intelligence.

scenario: You say you will send the photo.
english: I will send you the photo.
cultureNote: Offer to share photos after taking them.

scenario: You say you will remember this day.
cultureNote: This makes the moment emotionally meaningful.

scenario: You say you came for lunch with them.
english: I came to have lunch with you.
cultureNote: Food is a central social moment.

scenario: You say you are happy to be with them.
english: I am happy to be with you.
cultureNote: This is simple and emotionally useful.

scenario: You praise the food.
cultureNote: Praise should be direct and sincere.

scenario: You ask them to explain slowly.
cultureNote: Families may speak fast; asking slowly is normal.

scenario: You say goodbye and thank them.
cultureNote: End a family meal with thanks, not silence.

scenario: You ask if they can come tomorrow.
cultureNote: Invitations are often flexible; inshallah may mean maybe.

scenario: You invite them to your place.
english: You are invited to my place.
cultureNote: Even if symbolic, invitation expresses reciprocity.

scenario: You suggest meeting in a café.
english: Can we meet in a café?
cultureNote: Cafés are neutral and easy social spaces.

scenario: You ask what time is good.
cultureNote: Always let locals help choose a workable time.

scenario: You confirm the meeting place.
cultureNote: Clear confirmation prevents confusion.

scenario: You say you will come at six.
cultureNote: Time planning is practical in social visits.

scenario: You say you are a little late.
cultureNote: Apologize early if late.

scenario: You apologize for being late.
cultureNote: A short apology is enough.

scenario: You ask where to meet.
cultureNote: Useful for family outings or visits.

scenario: You ask if they arrived.
cultureNote: Works in WhatsApp or phone calls.

scenario: You say you brought a gift.
cultureNote: A small gift can be appropriate when visiting a home.

scenario: You say it is just something small.
english: It is just a small thing.
cultureNote: Modesty makes the gift feel natural.

scenario: You hope they like it.
cultureNote: Do not make the gift feel like a show.

scenario: You ask where to put it.
cultureNote: Practical and polite after entering.

scenario: You say enjoy it.
cultureNote: Bssaha is used for food, gifts, and new things.

scenario: You ask where they are now.
cultureNote: Use gender/context awareness later; draft needs native QA.

scenario: You say you are near the café.
cultureNote: Useful for meeting friends outside.

scenario: You say you are coming to them now.
english: I am coming to you now.
cultureNote: A simple movement phrase helps coordination.

scenario: You ask if the service is finished.
cultureNote: For shops, errands, or cafés, service timing matters.

scenario: You suggest going together.
cultureNote: This is friendly and collaborative.

scenario: You say you are joking.
cultureNote: Humor needs clear tone across cultures.

scenario: You ask them to speak slowly.
cultureNote: This lowers pressure while learning.

scenario: You say you did not understand the joke.
english: I did not understand the joke.
cultureNote: Good for real social learning.

scenario: You ask them to repeat it.
cultureNote: Repetition is essential in social listening.

scenario: You say they are very funny.
cultureNote: Compliment lightly; avoid sarcasm.

scenario: You say you do not want to talk about this topic.
arabic: ما بغيتش نهضر على هاد الموضوع
english: I do not want to talk about this topic.
cultureNote: Some topics can be sensitive; keep it respectful.

scenario: You suggest changing the conversation.
cultureNote: A soft redirect protects the relationship.

scenario: You say this is private.
cultureNote: Privacy can be expressed without anger.

scenario: You ask to talk later.
cultureNote: Useful when you need time or clarity.

scenario: You say you are comfortable like this.
cultureNote: Set boundaries calmly and respectfully.

scenario: You say it is good to be with friends.
english: It is good to be with friends.
cultureNote: Level 6 expands from tourist survival to relationships.

scenario: You say you speak slowly.
cultureNote: A learner can ask for patience without shame.

scenario: You say we laugh a little.
cultureNote: Humor is social, not only language.

scenario: You say you respect the family.
cultureNote: Respect is the core of family-level Darija.

scenario: You thank them for everything.
cultureNote: A flexible closing phrase for many social scenes.

scenario: You ask where the wedding will be.
cultureNote: Use only when invited or context is clear.

scenario: You ask if you should come early.
cultureNote: Event timing can be flexible; ask clearly.

scenario: You ask what to wear.
cultureNote: Clothing depends on family/event context.

scenario: You ask whether to buy a gift.
cultureNote: A gift may be thoughtful, not always required.

scenario: You say congratulations.
cultureNote: Mabrouk works for many happy events.

scenario: You simply congratulate someone.
cultureNote: Universal and very useful.

scenario: You wish things finish well.
cultureNote: Common for engagements, weddings, projects, and plans.

scenario: You say you are happy for them.
cultureNote: A warm phrase for friends and family.

scenario: You respond to a blessing.
cultureNote: Useful reply when someone says mabrouk.

scenario: You wish health and comfort.
cultureNote: Use for food, homes, clothes, gifts, and good news.

scenario: You apologize for arriving late.
cultureNote: Direct apology is better than over-explaining.

scenario: You thank them for patience.
cultureNote: Useful when people waited for you.

scenario: You say you did not mean it.
cultureNote: Helps repair misunderstandings.

scenario: You say you will make it up.
english: I will make it up to you.
cultureNote: Use carefully; it can imply future action.

scenario: You welcome someone back safely.
cultureNote: A very Moroccan phrase for return or recovery.

scenario: You ask them to send the location.
cultureNote: Mixed Darija/French/English tech words are normal.

scenario: You say you will send a message.
english: I will send you a message.
cultureNote: Useful for follow-up after meeting.

scenario: You say you heard the voice note.
cultureNote: Vocal is common for WhatsApp voice messages.

scenario: You say you did not hear well.
cultureNote: Practical for noisy streets or audio messages.

scenario: You ask them to repeat slowly.
cultureNote: Helps with real native-speed listening.

scenario: You ask what they recommend.
english: What do you recommend for me?
cultureNote: Locals often appreciate being asked for advice.

scenario: You ask where to go today.
cultureNote: Use friends/family as cultural guides.

scenario: You ask if a place is good.
cultureNote: Helps choose safe and appropriate places.

scenario: You ask what to avoid.
cultureNote: A respectful safety question is useful for travelers.

scenario: You thank them for the advice.
cultureNote: Advice strengthens social trust.

scenario: You ask if you can help.
cultureNote: Offer gently; do not force help.

scenario: You ask if they need anything.
cultureNote: Useful in visits, errands, or family events.

scenario: You offer to bring water.
cultureNote: Simple help is practical and polite.

scenario: You ask if you should leave the door open.
english: Should I leave the door open?
cultureNote: Home-context micro phrases matter.

scenario: You say no problem.
cultureNote: A key social phrase across all levels.

scenario: You say you came with a friend.
cultureNote: Introduces social context clearly.

scenario: You say you greeted the family.
cultureNote: This summarizes a culturally important action.

scenario: You say you drank tea.
cultureNote: Tea marks participation in hospitality.

scenario: You say you spoke slowly.
cultureNote: Confidence grows from simple speech.

scenario: You say you left politely.
cultureNote: Ending well is part of the social situation.

scenario: You greet everyone in a mixed gathering.
cultureNote: Plural greeting works for groups.

scenario: You thank them for the invitation.
cultureNote: A key sentence when entering any invitation.

scenario: You say the tea is beautiful.
cultureNote: Repeat warm hospitality language.

scenario: You say you must go now.
cultureNote: Announce departure gently, not suddenly.

scenario: You say we will meet soon.
cultureNote: A warm closing keeps the relationship open.

scenario: You begin the final social visit.
cultureNote: The final simulation starts with the right greeting.

scenario: You say you are happy to be with them.
english: I am happy to be with you all.
cultureNote: Emotional warmth is the goal of Level 6.

scenario: You say everything was good.
cultureNote: A simple summary phrase after a visit.

scenario: You thank them for hospitality.
cultureNote: This is the core of family/guest culture.

scenario: You close with a warm goodbye.
english: Goodbye, I will see you soon.
cultureNote: Final phrase confirms social connection, not only exit.
